channel.title: Home on Tyler Burleigh
channel.description: Recent content in Home on Tyler Burleigh

item.title: nutritionR: NLP nutrition analysis in R
item.description: I’m a bit of a nutrition nerd and I’ve been wanting to do some analysis of recipes that I cook a lot just because I’m curious about some of their properties. To get closer to that goal, I decided to write a package, nutritionR, that can perform a nutritional analysis of foods and recipes expressed in natural language. nutritionR does this by wrapping the Nutritionix API. It requires a free developer API key or above.

item.title: Yet Another Data Science Job Market Analysis
pyMTurkR (vignette): A HIT with a Qualification Test
item.description: Introduction Researchers are sometimes interested in recruiting people into a study who have certain traits or characteristics. For example, a researcher might be interested in surveying young adults between the ages of 18-24, or female executives over the age of 65. Targeted recruitment of participants is useful if you’re only interested in responses from a specific populations of individuals and not the “general population”. But it is also useful when you want to oversample a class of participants in your study who were underrepresented in a different sample.

item.title: pyMTurkR: An R package for MTurk Requesters
item.description: I’ve been working for a few months on this package called pyMTurkR. It’s an R package to interface with MTurk’s Requester API, replacing the now defunct MTurkR package. I’ve used MTurk for convenience sampling in many of my social science research, and when I use MTurk I like to use R to automate tasks like granting bonuses, approving assignments, etc. I didn’t want to see R lose support for the MTurk, so as soon as I heard the API on which MTurkR was based was going away and MTurkR would no longer work, I decided to roll up my sleeves and build a replacement.

item.title: Tracking R package downloads on Github
item.description: As a package author, it’s nice to know how often your package is getting installed and used. This is not only good for staying motivated (assuming your package is getting some use), but it’s also important as part of building a portfolio in data science. After all, data science is all about measurement and quantification. What better evidence is there of your contributions to Open Source Software than cold hard numbers that represent usage data?

item.title: What is fair payment on MTurk?
item.description: My go-to place for convenience sampling is MTurk. It’s fast, it’s easy, it’s flexible, it can be as simple or complicated as you need it to be, the data is high quality and the samples are fairly “representative” of the US population. But what is “fair payment” for “work” on MTurk? Objectively, if we accept that MTurk work is like other kinds of work, then we should aim to pay an amount that equates to a minimum hourly wage.

item.title: Surviving the Titanic with R caret
item.description: Introduction I’ve been meaning to “upskill” into the world of machine learning for some time. I’m no stranger to statistics. I was trained as a behavioral scientist; I know my way around a linear regression! But at the same time, I tend to use statistics to test formal hypotheses, and this seems (at least to an outsider like me) pretty different from machine learning. I want to learn new skills that will help me be a better data scientist, and machine learning seemes like a good place to start.

item.title: Writings
item.description: I’ve authored peer-reviewed scholarly articles and white papers. As of 2019-09-21, my papers were cited 299 times and my h-index was 8. (source: Google Scholar) Papers Uncanny Valley Burleigh, T. J. (2015). A challenge to the study of individual differences in uncanny valley sensitivity: The importance of looking at individual-level response patterns. Interaction Studies, 16(2). [PDF] Ferrey, A., Burleigh, T. J., & Fenske, M. (2015). Stimulus-category competition, inhibition and affective devaluation: A novel account of the Uncanny Valley.
